text.title: Property-Based Tests From LLM-Inferred Invariants Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the test-property-based-llm-invariants methodology plus the `skip-this-methodology` safety rule. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Every proposed invariant MUST cite the source text (docstring line, type annotation, or example call) that justifies it. Ungrounded invariants are rejected.
rule.rationale: Ungrounded properties hallucinate behaviour; grounding pins each property to a falsifiable claim in the source.

rule.statement: The agent MUST consider the five canonical families (round-trip, idempotence, oracle, metamorphic, monotonicity) and explicitly mark which apply and which do not (with reason).
rule.rationale: Without an exhaustive sweep, agents default to one family (usually round-trip) and miss the easy wins.

rule.statement: Property-based tests MUST be written before example-based tests. Example tests are reserved for documenting failing-case minimizations the property test produces.
rule.rationale: Example tests written first anchor the agent's thinking on specific inputs; properties anchor it on general claims.

rule.statement: Each property test MUST pin its random seed and shrink budget so failures are reproducible in CI. Floating seeds are forbidden in committed tests.
rule.rationale: Floating seeds turn a real failure into a flaky one nobody can reproduce.

rule.statement: Property-based tests MUST only target pure functions. Impure functions go through example-based or contract tests, not property tests.
rule.rationale: Property tests over impure code generate spurious failures (network errors, race conditions) that pollute the signal.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the preconditions in `## Applies If` are not all true, or any item in `## Skip If` holds. The decision tree routes to this rule on those branches.
rule.rationale: A methodology that runs on the wrong input wastes tokens and produces misleading artifacts. The explicit skip rule is the safety valve.
